label: ai
topic: Students should openly express their political views and engage in debate on campus
text:
I grew up in a place where political identity wasn't something you debated; it was something you inherited. In my hometown, most people voted the same way, watched the same news, and shared a similar sense of what was "normal." Coming to a liberal university felt like stepping into a completely different world. Suddenly, the assumptions I'd taken for granted were being questioned from every direction, and conversations that would have been unremarkable back home felt charged with meaning.

The 2024 presidential election made that contrast impossible to ignore. On campus, political discussions were everywhere—classrooms, dining halls, even group chats that were supposedly about homework. But what struck me wasn't just the intensity of those conversations; it was how often they stayed within certain boundaries. People spoke passionately, but usually to audiences that already agreed with them. There was a sense of openness, but also an undercurrent of caution, like everyone was aware of the social risks of saying the "wrong" thing.

For me, that created a strange kind of tension. I didn't fully align with the dominant views on campus, but I also didn't feel entirely at home with the perspectives I grew up around. It left me in this in-between space, where speaking up felt both necessary and uncomfortable. I remember one class discussion where the conversation turned to a policy issue that hit close to home. I hesitated before saying anything, not because I didn't have an opinion, but because I was trying to predict how it would be received.

When I did speak, the reaction wasn't hostile, but it was clear that I was outside the consensus. A few people engaged thoughtfully, asking questions and pushing back in ways that felt productive. Others stayed quiet, and I couldn't tell whether they disagreed or just didn't want to get involved. Walking out of that class, I kept replaying the conversation in my head, wondering if I'd said too much or not enough.

Experiences like that have made me think a lot about what it actually means to have open dialogue on campus. It's easy to say that we value diverse perspectives, but that only matters if people feel comfortable expressing those perspectives in the first place. And comfort doesn't mean the absence of disagreement—it means knowing that disagreement won't automatically lead to dismissal or isolation.

At the same time, I've also seen how conversations can go wrong. There are moments when discussions turn into performances, where the goal shifts from understanding to winning. In those situations, people retreat into defensiveness, and the possibility of meaningful exchange disappears. It's frustrating, but it's also a reminder that open dialogue requires effort from everyone involved.

What I've come to believe is that we need to be more willing to sit with discomfort. Not the kind that comes from being attacked or disrespected, but the kind that comes from encountering ideas that challenge our assumptions. That means asking questions instead of making assumptions about where someone is coming from. It means being willing to explain your own views without expecting immediate agreement. And it means recognizing that changing someone's mind is a long process, not a single conversation.

Some of the most valuable discussions I've had on campus haven't ended with consensus. They've ended with a better understanding of why someone thinks the way they do, even if I still disagree. Those moments don't make headlines, but they matter. They're the foundation of a campus culture where people can engage honestly without feeling like they have to hide parts of themselves.

Being a student from a deep red state at a liberal university has forced me to confront my own assumptions as much as anyone else's. It's been uncomfortable at times, but it's also been one of the most important parts of my education. If we're serious about preparing students for a complex, divided world, then we need to create spaces where those kinds of conversations can happen—not just safely, but genuinely.
